title: Adding lawn clippings to a compost heap
subtitle: How to use grass without creating an anaerobic mat
excerpt: Fresh lawn clippings are one of the best free nitrogen activators for a compost heap, but added in thick layers they compact into an airless mat that blocks decomposition and smells. The fix is proportion and layering.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic guidance on grass clippings in compost. HDRA technical notes on green waste management. RHS growing notes on lawn management and compost inputs.

glossaryTerms.definition: The two categories of compost material. Browns are carbon-rich dry materials: cardboard, straw, woodchip, shredded paper. Greens are nitrogen-rich wet materials: grass clippings, vegetable peelings, coffee grounds, fresh manure.

Decomposition that occurs without oxygen. Anaerobic breakdown is slower than aerobic decomposition, produces foul-smelling compounds including hydrogen sulphide and ammonia, and is the main cause of a slimy, smelly compost heap.

content.text: Fresh grass clippings contain around 3-4 percent nitrogen by dry weight, making them one of the most effective free nitrogen sources available to a home composter. The problem is physical structure: cut grass blades are long, flat, and slightly sticky. Added in a thick layer they matt together within hours, excluding air and creating
conditions that produce a grey-green slime and a strong ammonia or sulphur smell. The nitrogen is still there, but it is being lost to the air rather than feeding the heap.
Add clippings in layers no deeper than 50-75 mm, immediately followed by an equal volume of

: torn cardboard, shredded paper, dry leaves, or woodchip. The bulky brown material creates air pockets between the grass blades and absorbs the excess moisture that drives anaerobic conditions. Repeat the pattern with every mowing rather than banking up large quantities between additions.
When you have more clippings than the heap can absorb
After the first main mow of the year, a medium-sized lawn produces far more clippings than a modestly-sized heap can balance with available browns. Options in order of effectiveness:
Spread excess clippings as a mulch around garden beds to a depth of 50-75 mm. They decompose in place, suppress weeds, and feed the soil without passing through the heap.

Dry the surplus in a thin layer on a path or hard surface for two to three days. Dried grass becomes a brown material: the nitrogen content drops but the structure opens up and the material can be added to the heap in larger quantities without matting.

Leave the clippings on the lawn (grasscycling). A mower that mulches returns finely chopped clippings directly to the turf, where they decompose within a few days and return around a third of the lawn's nitrogen needs. This is the most efficient use of the clippings from a soil-nutrition standpoint.

Clippings from a lawn that was recently treated with a selective herbicide or lawn weedkiller. Most UK lawn herbicides persist through the composting process and can damage vegetable garden plants. Wait at least four mowings after herbicide application before adding clippings to a food-garden compost heap.
Clippings from a lawn dominated by coarse annual grasses that have headed up and set seed. The seed will survive a cold heap and germinate in the finished compost.

If the heap already has a compacted grass layer, break it up with a fork, add a thick layer of torn cardboard or shredded paper over and through it, and turn the heap to mix. The heap should begin to heat within 24-48 hours once air is restored.
